title: How the body develops chemical sensitivity after prolonged stress
author: Kalesh

The day the world started smelling like poison 

 I remember the exact moment I realized something was wrong with my body. I was walking past a department...
htmlContent:
The day the world started smelling like poison

I remember the exact moment I realized something was wrong with my body. I was walking past a department store, and the perfume sample sprayed on a card hit me like a physical blow. My throat tightened. My eyes started watering. My chest felt like someone was sitting on it. And I thought: What the hell is happening to me?

I used to walk through perfume sections without a second thought. I used to wear cologne. I used to light candles. But after years of chronic stress, after my nervous system had been stuck in survival mode for what felt like forever, my body started reacting to things it never reacted to before. Perfume. Cleaning products. Diesel fumes. The smell of someone's laundry detergent walking past me on the street.

If this sounds familiar, you're not crazy. And you're not making it up.

Here's the thing. What you're experiencing is real. It has a mechanism. It has a name. And it has everything to do with what your nervous system has been through.

What happens when your nervous system becomes a smoke detector that's always on

Let's talk about Stephen Porges for a moment. He's the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, and his work explains so much about what's happening in your body. Porges talks about something called "neuroception" - the way your nervous system scans for safety and danger without you even thinking about it. It happens below the level of conscious awareness. Your body is constantly asking: Am I safe? Is this person safe? Is this environment safe?

Under normal circumstances, your neuroception is flexible. It can tell the difference between a real threat (a car swerving toward you) and a false alarm (someone wearing strong perfume). But when you've been under prolonged stress, your neuroception gets recalibrated. It starts treating everything as a potential threat. Your nervous system becomes a smoke detector that's permanently set to high sensitivity. It goes off when you burn toast. It goes off when someone boils water. It goes off when there's no smoke at all.

Think about it. Your body has been through so much. Maybe it was years of emotional abuse. Maybe it was a traumatic childhood. Maybe it was chronic work stress, financial instability, or living in a body that never felt safe. Whatever it was, your nervous system learned that the world is dangerous. And it started protecting you the only way it knows how: by making you hypervigilant to every possible threat, including invisible ones in the air.

This is where chemical sensitivity comes from. It's not that your body has suddenly developed an allergy to everything. It's that your nervous system has started flagging certain smells, chemicals, and environmental inputs as dangerous. Your immune system joins the party too, because your body is now in a state of high alert. Mast cells, which are part of your immune response, start degranulating more easily. Histamine gets released. Inflammation goes up. And suddenly, you're reacting to things that never bothered you before.

Wild, right? Your body is trying to protect you from a threat that isn't there. But it doesn't know that. It just knows it needs to keep you alive, and this is the best strategy it's got.

The chemistry of chronic stress and why your body starts fighting itself

Let's get a little more specific about what's happening inside your cells. When you're under chronic stress, your adrenal glands pump out cortisol and adrenaline. These are survival hormones. They're designed to help you run from a predator or fight for your life. But when the stress doesn't stop, when you're stuck in that state for months or years, your body starts to break down.

High cortisol over long periods suppresses your immune system in some ways and overactivates it in others. It makes you more susceptible to inflammation. It changes how your gut microbiome functions. It alters the permeability of your blood-brain barrier. And it affects something called the "mast cell" system, which I mentioned earlier. Mast cells are like little security guards stationed throughout your body. When they sense a threat, they release histamine and other inflammatory chemicals. Under chronic stress, these guards get trigger-happy. They start reacting to things that aren't actually dangerous. Perfume. Mold. Pollen. Even certain foods.

Gabor Mate, the physician and author who wrote extensively about the connection between stress and illness, talks about this in his work. He describes how the body's responses to stress are not separate from its responses to the environment. They're all connected. The same nervous system that's regulating your heart rate and your digestion is also regulating your immune system and your inflammatory responses. When one part of that system is out of balance, everything else gets pulled along with it.

I remember sitting in my doctor's office, listing all the things I was now reacting to, and feeling like I was describing someone else's life. I couldn't use scented laundry detergent anymore. I couldn't walk through the cleaning aisle at the grocery store. I had to ask friends not to wear perfume when I visited. And I felt so ashamed. Like I was being dramatic. Like I was making it up.

But I wasn't. And neither are you.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Mate's work helped me understand that my body wasn't betraying me. It was trying to communicate something I hadn't been able to say with words. The chemical sensitivity wasn't a random malfunction. It was a message.

How the nervous system recalibrates and what you can actually do about it

So here's the part where I tell you there's hope. Because there is. Your nervous system can recalibrate. It can learn to lower that smoke detector sensitivity. But it takes time, and it takes the right kind of input.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium is involved in over 300 biochemical reactions in your body, and it in regulating the nervous system. Glycinate form is particularly good for calming anxiety and supporting sleep. I started taking it at night, and within a few weeks, I noticed my startle response was less intense. I wasn't jumping at every little sound. My body felt a little less on edge.

Another tool that helped me was using deep pressure stimulation. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The weight provides a sense of grounding and safety. It activates your parasympathetic nervous system, the part that helps you rest and digest. I started sleeping under mine, and it made a real difference in how reactive I felt during the day.

But supplements and blankets aren't the whole story. The real work is in teaching your nervous system that it's safe now. That the danger has passed. That you don't need to be on high alert anymore.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, developed a whole approach to trauma recovery that's built on this idea. He talks about how trauma gets stored in the body, and how you can gently release it by paying attention to physical sensations. You don't have to relive the trauma. You just have to notice what your body is feeling, without judgment, and allow it to complete the responses that got frozen in time.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Levine's book walks you through how the body holds trauma and how you can begin to release it. It's not a quick fix. It's not a five-step plan. But it's honest about what this work actually requires.

Stay with me here. Because I know this is a lot. And I know that when you're in the middle of it, when you're reacting to things and feeling like your body is out of control, it's easy to lose hope. But your body is not your enemy. It's trying to protect you. It's doing the best it can with the information it has.

The way forward is not to fight your body. It's to listen to it. To give it what it needs. To slowly, gently, show it that the world is not as dangerous as it thinks.

What recovery looks like and why it's not linear

I want to be honest with you about what recovery looks like. It's not a straight line. You don't wake up one day and suddenly stop reacting to everything. There are good days and bad days. There are setbacks. There are times when you feel like you're back at square one.

And that's okay.

What I've learned is that healing your nervous system is like training a muscle. You can't go to the gym once and expect to be strong. You have to show up again and again, even when it's hard, even when you don't see results right away. The same is true for your nervous system. Every time you practice grounding. Every time you take a deep breath instead of reacting. Every time you give yourself permission to rest. You're sending a signal to your body: We're safe. We can relax.

Over time, those signals add up. The smoke detector starts to settle down. The mast cells calm their tits, as my therapist once put it. You start to notice that you can be in a room with someone wearing perfume and not feel like you're going to die. You start to trust your body again.

But it takes time. And it takes patience. And it takes a lot of self-compassion.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Maybe you felt a little relief. Maybe you felt resistance. Maybe you felt nothing at all. That's okay. Just notice it. That's the beginning.

Your body is not broken. It's been through a lot. And it's trying to tell you something. The question is: are you ready to listen?

I don't have a neat ending for this article. Because there isn't one. This is ongoing work. This is the messy, beautiful, frustrating, hopeful process of coming home to your body. And I'm right here with you, doing the same thing.

You're not alone in this.
